text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY / RADIATION ONCOLOGY JOINT SURVEILLANCE NOTE
HPV-Positive Oropharyngeal Squamous Cell Carcinoma

ATTENDING PHYSICIANS: Medical Oncology, Radiation Oncology (Dr. Jennifer Park)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Routine 18-month post-treatment surveillance visit for HPV-positive right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma, status post definitive concurrent chemoradiation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Margaret Chen is a 53-year-old Chinese-American female, never-smoker, with a history of HPV-positive (p16-positive, HPV-16 ISH-positive) moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, staged as T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition. She initially presented approximately 18 months ago with a 3-month history of right-sided throat discomfort and odynophagia, ultimately diagnosed via direct laryngoscopy and biopsy. Work-up at that time confirmed a 3.1 cm right base of tongue primary with a single ipsilateral right level IIA lymph node at 2.4 cm, no extranodal extension, no distant metastases on PET/CT staging.

She was treated with definitive intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) using a simultaneous integrated boost technique — 70 Gy to the primary and involved node in 35 fractions, with elective nodal volumes to 59.5 Gy — delivered concurrently with cisplatin 100 mg/m2 IV every 3 weeks, two cycles completed at full dose. Treatment course was completed without interruption, though complicated by grade 3 mucositis requiring PEG tube placement in week 5 of treatment, grade 2 radiation dermatitis, and grade 2 fatigue. Following treatment completion, she achieved a complete metabolic response on her 12-week post-treatment PET/CT (previously SUVmax 12.4 at primary, now background-level uptake with no FDG-avid nodal disease). Clinical exam confirmed no visible or palpable residual disease at that time.

Subsequent surveillance has been reassuring throughout. At the 6-month mark, she had returned to part-time teaching, her PEG tube had been removed, and her weight was nearly back to baseline. At 12 months, CT neck demonstrated stable post-radiation changes with no mass lesion, no abnormal enhancement, and the right IIA node decreased to 0.8 cm consistent with treatment effect and fibrosis. Audiogram showed mild high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss at 4–8 kHz bilaterally attributed to cisplatin, not yet requiring amplification. She was noted to have radiation-induced hypothyroidism, and her levothyroxine was uptitrated with subsequent normalization of TSH.

She presents today for her 18-month surveillance visit. She reports feeling very well overall. She has returned to full-time work as a high school music teacher and is engaged in her full professional and social activities. She denies any new neck masses, throat pain, hemoptysis, unexplained weight loss, otalgia, or neurologic symptoms. She has no concerns about disease recurrence. Her main residual complaint is persistent dry mouth, which she describes as manageable.

Review of today's imaging: CT neck with contrast performed in advance of today's visit was reviewed collaboratively by the medical oncology and radiation oncology teams and shows stable post-radiation changes in the oropharynx without any new mass, abnormal mucosal enhancement, or pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. The right level IIA node has decreased further to 0.6 cm, stable-to-decreased from the prior scan. Findings are fully consistent with durable treatment response without recurrence.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

- Constitutional: Denies fever, chills, night sweats. Weight stable. Energy good.
- Head/Eyes/ENT: Persistent xerostomia, grade 1. Sipping water frequently. No new sore throat. No new neck mass. Mild bilateral tinnitus noted occasionally, stable. No otalgia. No epistaxis or hemoptysis.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or edema.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea or cough.
- Gastrointestinal: Good appetite. No dysphagia with regular diet; mild difficulty with very dry or crusty bread textures related to xerostomia. Bowel habits normal.
- Genitourinary: No complaints.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild neck stiffness with prolonged postures (e.g., during teaching), managed with stretching exercises.
- Neurologic: No focal neurologic symptoms. No headache.
- Endocrine: Hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine; TSH last checked and within normal limits per PCP.
- Skin: Residual hyperpigmentation of the bilateral anterior neck skin from radiation, stable and not bothersome.
- Psychiatric: Mood good. Some residual anxiety around surveillance visits ("scanxiety"), which she discusses openly and manages with mindfulness practices.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (as previously documented; no new interval diagnoses)
- HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC, right base of tongue, T2N1M0 — treated as above
- Radiation-induced hypothyroidism (post-treatment)
- Pre-existing hypothyroidism (levothyroxine dose subsequently increased)
- Seasonal allergic rhinitis
- Cisplatin-associated mild bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss

MEDICATIONS
- Levothyroxine (dose adjusted post-radiation, current dose per PCP)
- Pilocarpine 5 mg three times daily (for xerostomia, partial benefit per patient)
- Artificial saliva spray (Biotene) as needed
- Loratadine 10 mg daily as needed for allergic rhinitis
- Daily high-fluoride prescription toothpaste with custom fluoride trays (dental oncology recommendation)
- Calcium/vitamin D supplementation

ALLERGIES: NKDA (no known drug allergies)

SOCIAL HISTORY: Never-smoker. No alcohol use. Works full-time as a high school music teacher; returned to full-time work approximately 3 months ago. Chinese-American ethnicity. Active lifestyle, resumes regular walking and exercise.

FAMILY HISTORY: No family history of head and neck cancer. No significant family cancer history reported.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished female in no acute distress. Pleasant and communicative. Appears stated age.

Vitals: Weight stable compared to prior visits; near pre-treatment baseline. Blood pressure within normal limits. Heart rate regular.

Head and Neck:
- Oropharyngeal mucosa: Mild residual mucosal pallor and subtle atrophy of the right base of tongue region consistent with post-radiation changes. No mucosal ulceration, no visible mass, no friable tissue, no suspicious white or red lesions. Hypopharynx clear to indirect visualization.
- Flexible nasopharyngoscopy (performed in coordination with ENT): Well-healed mucosa at the right base of tongue. No exophytic lesion, submucosal fullness, or mass effect. Epiglottis and arytenoids appear normal. Vocal cord mobility symmetric and intact bilaterally. Post-radiation mucosal changes present as expected — pale, slightly atrophic mucosa without signs of neoplastic recurrence. No pooling of secretions.
- Neck: Supple. No palpable lymphadenopathy in the bilateral levels I–V. Right level IIA region without palpable mass (consistent with imaging showing residual small fibrotic node at 0.6 cm). No tenderness on palpation. Mild soft tissue firmness in the bilateral anterior neck consistent with post-radiation fibrosis. Carotid pulses present and symmetric, no bruits auscultated.
- Mouth: Mild xerostomia apparent on examination. Mucous membranes slightly dry. No dental caries identified on inspection; patient adherent to fluoride tray protocol per report. No trismus; mouth opens fully without restriction.
- Skin of the neck: Bilateral hyperpigmentation and mild telangiectasia overlying the radiation field, stable, no evidence of wound breakdown or moist desquamation.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. No organomegaly.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. Shoulder shrug symmetric. No evidence of cranial neuropathy.

---

RELEVANT LABORATORY / DIAGNOSTIC DATA

- TSH: Within normal limits (per PCP records, on optimized levothyroxine dose)
- CBC and comprehensive metabolic panel: Not repeated at this routine surveillance visit per protocol; will reassess at annual visit if clinically indicated
- Audiologic follow-up: Annual pure-tone audiometry recommended; most recent audiogram at 12 months showed mild high-frequency (4–8 kHz) sensorineural hearing loss bilaterally, stable, below threshold for intervention
- Imaging: CT neck with contrast reviewed (see above, 18-month post-treatment surveillance scan — no evidence of recurrence)

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-POSITIVE OROPHARYNGEAL SCC, RIGHT BASE OF TONGUE, T2N1M0 — COMPLETE RESPONSE, NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE AT 18 MONTHS POST-TREATMENT

   Ms. Chen continues to demonstrate an excellent, durable response to definitive chemoradiation. Today's clinical examination and review of 18-month CT neck are entirely consistent with durable complete remission. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy confirms well-healed mucosa without evidence of recurrence at the primary site. No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. She remains fully functional with ECOG performance status 0.

   Prognosis discussion reinforced: HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC in a non-smoker achieving complete response after definitive chemoradiation carries a 5-year overall survival exceeding 85% in published series. She is now 18 months into that follow-up window with no signals of concern. We continue to be optimistic.

   Surveillance plan: Continue per NCCN guidelines for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC. History and physical with flexible nasopharyngoscopy every 2–3 months through year 3 (approximately 6 months from now for next visit). Cross-sectional imaging with CT neck (or PET/CT if clinical concern arises) at clinically directed intervals — next planned imaging at 24 months. Patient counseled to present immediately for evaluation if she develops any new neck mass, persistent throat pain, hemoptysis, otalgia, or other concerning symptoms between scheduled visits.

2. XEROSTOMIA, GRADE 1 (PERSISTENT POST-RADIATION)

   Expected late effect of parotid gland irradiation, present and stable. Well-managed with pilocarpine 5 mg TID and Biotene spray. Patient does not find this significantly limiting to her quality of life. Reviewed importance of dental hygiene, regular dental visits, and continued use of prescription fluoride trays daily, as radiation-induced xerostomia increases the risk of dental caries and, rarely, osteoradionecrosis. Discussed avoidance of dental extractions within the radiation field if at all possible going forward; if required, should be coordinated with her dental oncology team.

3. CISPLATIN-ASSOCIATED OTOTOXICITY

   Mild bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss stable at last audiogram. Does not currently require hearing aids. Annual audiologic monitoring recommended. Patient instructed to avoid excessive noise exposure and ototoxic medications where possible.

4. RADIATION-INDUCED HYPOTHYROIDISM

   TSH normalized on current levothyroxine dose. Continue annual TSH monitoring; further dose adjustment per PCP and/or endocrinology as needed. Patient aware of the chronic nature of this post-radiation effect.

5. CAROTID ARTERY SURVEILLANCE (LATE RADIATION EFFECT)

   Discussed that irradiation of the neck carries a long-term risk of accelerated carotid atherosclerosis. Patient does not have other major cardiovascular risk factors (non-smoker, no known hypertension, no diabetes). Baseline carotid duplex ultrasound recommended at 5 years post-treatment per institutional approach. Cardiovascular risk factor optimization ongoing.

6. DYSPHAGIA / SWALLOWING FUNCTION

   No clinically significant dysphagia at present. Patient is on a full regular diet with minor behavioral accommodations (avoids very dry textures). No further formal SLP visits indicated at this time; patient instructed to continue independent swallowing exercises. Will re-engage SLP if swallowing deteriorates.

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL / QUALITY OF LIFE

   Patient doing very well psychosocially. Returned to full-time professional activities and reports high quality of life. Mild surveillance-related anxiety acknowledged; she is managing this effectively with mindfulness. Supportive care resources discussed and made available if needed.

Return to clinic in approximately 3 months for next surveillance visit. Patient will contact clinic with any interim concerns.

Time spent: Approximately 45 minutes including review of imaging, flexible nasopharyngoscopy, counseling, and documentation.
